citations.narrative:
At the goat enclosure near the parking area, the public is allowed to walk directly up to the primary enclosure that contains
9 domestic goats with no attendant or public barriers present. The primary enclosures’ fences and gates are
approximately 4 feet high with spacing that would allow hands or arms to reach through and touch the animals. During the
inspection the APHIS inspector observed multiple guests interacting with the goats without an attendant present.
Unattended public contact does not ensure safe public interaction with the animals and may lead to injury of the public
and/or the animals. The licensee must ensure that during periods of public exhibition, animals are handled with sufficient
distance and/or barriers between the animals and the general viewing public to help assure the safety of both the animals
and the public.
Correct by April 22, 2026

Unguarded electrical cords for heating elements were within the outdoor primary enclosures for 4 adult black-and-white
ruffed lemurs. The cords were not plugged into power at the time of inspection. Electrical cords within the primary
enclosure may result in adverse events such as electrocution, entanglement, or strangulation. Primary enclosures must
be constructed and maintained so that they protect the nonhuman primates from injury.
Correct by facility at the time of inspection April 7, 2026.

citations.desc: Environment enhancement to promote psychological well-being.
citations.narrative:
A review of the facility’s environmental enrichment plan for non-human primates does not include special considerations
for a singly housed red-handed tamarin. Individually housed nonhuman primates that are unable to see and hear other
nonhuman primates of their own or compatible species must be provided with special attention. The facility must develop
a written plan that takes this tamarin’s current circumstances into consideration. This includes providing an enhanced
environment to address its needs as directed by the attending veterinarian.
Correct by April 22, 2026

Three outdoor enclosures housing the palm cockatoo, blue-and-yellow macaw and white-tailed hawk do not provide
shelter from the wind. These enclosures consist of chain link fencing on all sides, with partial roof structures above. No
structures were present to obstruct or inhibit the impact of wind entering the enclosures.
Inappropriate shelter exposes birds to extreme weather conditions which could cause discomfort, illness, or distress to the
animals. The facility must provide an adequate shelter to protect the birds from any adverse weather conditions that is
large enough to comfortably contain all the birds at one time and prevent their discomfort from direct sunlight,
precipitation, or wind.
Correct by April 22, 2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
n

narrative:
2.131(c)(1) 
   
Handling of animals. 
At the goat enclosure near the parking area, the public is allowed to walk directly up to the primary enclosure that contains 
9 domestic goats with no attendant or public barriers present. The primary enclosures’ fences and gates are 
approximately 4 feet high with spacing that would allow hands or arms to reach through and touch the animals. During the 
inspection the APHIS inspector observed multiple guests interacting with the goats without an attendant present. 
Unattended public contact does not ensure safe public interaction with the animals and may lead to injury of the public 
and/or the animals. The licensee must ensure that during periods of public exhibition, animals are handled with sufficient 
distance and/or barriers between the animals and the general viewing public to help assure the safety of both the animals 
and the public. 
Correct by April 22, 2026 
3.80(a)(2)(ii) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
Unguarded electrical cords for heating elements were within the outdoor primary enclosures for 4 adult black-and-white 
ruffed lemurs. The cords were not plugged into power at the time of inspection. Electrical cords within the primary 
enclosure may result in adverse events such as electrocution, entanglement, or strangulation. Primary enclosures must 
be constructed and maintained so that they protect the nonhuman primates from injury. 
 
Correct by facility at the time of inspection April 7, 2026. 
3.81(c)(4) 
   
Environment enhancement to promote psychological well-being. 
A review of the facility’s environmental enrichment plan for non-human primates does not include special considerations 
for a singly housed red-handed tamarin. Individually housed nonhuman primates that are unable to see and hear other 
nonhuman primates of their own or compatible species must be provided with special attention. The facility must develop 
a written plan that takes this tamarin’s current circumstances into consideration. This includes providing an enhanced 
environment to address its needs as directed by the attending veterinarian. 
Correct by April 22, 2026

3.152(b) 
   
Facilities, outdoor 
Three outdoor enclosures housing the palm cockatoo, blue-and-yellow macaw and white-tailed hawk do not provide 
shelter from the wind. These enclosures consist of chain link fencing on all sides, with partial roof structures above. No 
structures were present to obstruct or inhibit the impact of wind entering the enclosures.  
 
Inappropriate shelter exposes birds to extreme weather conditions which could cause discomfort, illness, or distress to the 
animals. The facility must provide an adequate shelter to protect the birds from any adverse weather conditions that is 
large enough to comfortably contain all the birds at one time and prevent their discomfort from direct sunlight, 
precipitation, or wind.  
Correct by April 22, 2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
